backward_pawns.title: Backward Pawns
description: A backward pawn is a pawn whose neighbouring pawns have advanced past it, meaning that it is the only pawn
amongst its neighbours which cannot be defended by another pawn in its current location.
Backward pawns are typically a
weakness; if they cannot be easily advanced, they must be guarded by pieces instead of by other pawns, which
necessitates tying those pieces down to their defence.
If a backward pawn is captured, its neighbouring pawns may also then become backward pawns as a consequence,
resulting in the larger
disintegrating as the weak spots multiply.
By its nature, a backward pawn also creates a
directly in front of it.

the_centre_pawns.title: The Centre Pawns
description: The centre pawns (those positioned in the d- and e-files, also referred to as the
and
respectively) are the two pawns capable of
the largest region of the
with each pawn able to cover 2 central squares apiece.
In contrast to controlling the centre from a distance with a knight or bishop, applying pawns to this task
constitutes a smaller
and comes with the additional benefits of creating
and forming a
into the opponent's territory.
The king's pawn has the unique trait that pushing it forward from its starting position simultaneously
clears a full
for
other pieces on their starting squares - the kingside bishop and the queen - making it the single best pawn
to push with the goal of accelerating
Pushing the queen's pawn clears the line of sight for the queenside bishop. While it does also partially
clear the forward path for the queen, the queen's mobility along this path is still very restricted and it
simply does not sufficiently benefit from being developed to d2 or d3 to make use of this clearance.
It is important to be aware that pushing this pawn also opens a diagonal
which can lead to
involving moves like
(by White)/
(by Black) to win
or even to play checkmate if players fail to take this danger into consideration
when making subsequent moves.
The king's pawn is more fragile than the queen's pawn when advanced into the centre; the king does not
guard it from a distance, and would in fact be endangered by the act of pushing
to support it from the flank.
This asymmetry means that while pushing the king's pawn is a strong move at
the very start of a match (since there is initially nothing in the centre to capture an undefended pawn), it
is significantly more difficult to play once the e4 (for White) or e5 (for Black) square is covered by the
opponent's pawns or pieces.
The queen's pawn, conversely, is far easier to push directly into tension with the opponent's pawn, particularly
if supported by
and is much more frequently played as a
in games which feature a contested centre.
